prompt:
Before solving this problem, analyze it:
(a) What type of problem is this?
(b) What cognitive strategy is most relevant?
(c) What will the hardest part be?
Then solve it.

Respond ONLY with JSON:
{"problem_type": "...", "strategy": "...", "hardest_part": "...", "solution": "..."}

Problem: A school district spends $2 million annually on a remedial math program
for students who score below grade level on standardized tests. The
program shows that 60% of enrolled students improve their scores the
following year. The district considers this a success and wants to
expand the program. Before expanding, what critical comparison is
missing from this evaluation? What statistical phenomenon might explain
the 60% improvement rate even if the program had zero effect?

parsed.problem_type: Statistical reasoning about causality, experimental design, and interpretation of improvement metrics
parsed.strategy: Identify missing control conditions and recognize regression to the mean as an alternative explanation for observed improvement without actual program effect
parsed.hardest_part: Understanding why students selected on the basis of being below-threshold naturally revert toward the population mean on the next measurement, creating apparent improvement independent of intervention
parsed.solution:
Critical missing comparison: A control group of students who scored below grade level but did NOT enroll in the program. Without this, we cannot attribute the 60% improvement to the program rather than other factors.

Statistical phenomenon: Regression to the mean. Students are selected for enrollment because they scored below grade level—an extreme performance. Standardized test scores contain random measurement error and natural variation. Students whose scores fell furthest below average were likely influenced by a combination of (1) true low ability and (2) random bad luck on test day. When these students retake the test the following year, the random component regresses back toward the population average, creating apparent improvement even if the program has zero effect.

Example parallel: If you identify all people experiencing severe back pain on a given day and provide a treatment, many will improve simply because they were measured at their worst point. The regression to their personal baseline mimics program efficacy.

Conclusion: The 60% figure is misleading without a control group comparison. The district must compare improvement rates between (1) students who received the program vs. (2) similar students who did not, to isolate the actual program effect from the statistical artifact of regression to the mean.
